title: RAP Unmanaged Scenario — Expert Implementation
description: Manual implementation of every RAP operation. When to choose unmanaged, custom CRUD implementation, locking patterns, transaction control, draft hand-written implementation, and integration with legacy BAPIs/RFCs.

sections.title: When to Choose Unmanaged

description.value:
The choice between RAP managed and unmanaged scenario is one of the most consequential architectural decisions in modern ABAP development. Managed scenario provides a framework that implements CREATE, UPDATE, DELETE, and draft handling automatically. Unmanaged scenario requires you to implement every operation manually. The tradeoff is developer productivity vs architectural flexibility.

Choose Unmanaged when: (1) Custom persistence — the business object data is not stored in a single database table but across multiple legacy tables, views, or even external systems. You need to control exactly how data is written. (2) Integration with existing BAPIs/RFCs — if you must call BAPI or RFC function modules for create/update/delete operations, managed scenario cannot support this. (3) Complex locking logic — the standard RAP locking (based on CDS entity) is insufficient. You need custom lock object calls with complex lock parameter logic. (4) Transaction control — you need explicit COMMIT WORK / ROLLBACK WORK control, or you need to participate in an existing LUW. Managed scenario controls the transaction boundary. (5) Non-standard draft behavior — the managed draft lifecycle does not fit your business process. For example, you need to prevent draft creation for certain users, or you need to enforce unique constraints across draft and active records.

Choose Managed when: Your business object maps cleanly to CDS views backed by database tables, standard draft handling is acceptable, and you want maximum developer productivity. For 80% of use cases, managed scenario is the right choice. For the remaining 20%, unmanaged provides the necessary escape valve.

decisionTree.prompt: Should I choose unmanaged RAP for this business object?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Does your business object require custom persistence, legacy BAPI integration, or non-standard draft behavior?" Answer yes to any = unmanaged candidate. Otherwise, start with managed and migrate only when you hit framework limits.

branches.condition: Data spans multiple legacy tables or external systems
branches.path: Unmanaged. Custom persistence requires manual CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE. The framework cannot auto-generate SQL for multi-table or external persistence.

branches.condition: Must wrap existing BAPIs/RFCs with standard commit behavior
branches.path: Unmanaged. BAPIs call COMMIT WORK internally, breaking RAP's managed transaction assumption. Implement BAPI calls in create/update methods, handle double-commit prevention via set_update_state.

branches.condition: Standard CRUD on single CDS-backed table with simple validations
branches.path: Managed. Use managed scenario. Save ~80% boilerplate. Unmanaged is 10x more code for no additional benefit.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Unmanaged is the escape valve for the 20% of entities that don't fit the managed pattern. Start managed, migrate to unmanaged only when you hit a concrete framework limitation.

sections.title: Manual CRUD Implementation

description.value:
In unmanaged scenario, you must implement the CREATE, UPDATE, and DELETE methods manually. The framework does not write to the database for you — it calls your methods and expects you to handle persistence. The contract between the framework and your implementation is defined by the parameter interfaces of these methods.

CREATE Contract: The framework passes the data to be created in the entities parameter. You must: (1) Validate the data (authorization, field validity, business rules). (2) Assign an ID (if early numbering, done by the framework; if late, you must generate). (3) Write the data to the database (or external system). (4) Map %cid to actual keys in the mapped table. (5) Handle errors via failed and reported.

UPDATE Contract: The framework passes the entities with the changed fields marked in %control. You must: (1) Read the current state from the database. (2) Apply only the changed fields (check %control-<field> = if_abap_behv=>mk-on). (3) Validate the updated state. (4) Persist the changes.

DELETE Contract: You must: (1) Check if deletion is allowed (referential integrity, business rules). (2) Delete the database record(s). (3) Handle cascading deletes for child entities.

Staff+ Insight: The unmanaged CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE methods are the command handlers in CQRS terminology. They receive commands, validate them, and persist events. This separation is architecturally cleaner than the classic ABAP pattern of mixing read and write logic in the same function module.

CLASS zbp_rap_unmanaged IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD create FOR INSERT
    IMPORTING entities FOR ComplexBO.

    DATA: ls_db TYPE zcomplexbo.

    LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(ls_entity).
      " Copy data from RAP entity to DB structure
      MOVE-CORRESPONDING ls_entity TO ls_db.

      " Handle early numbering (ID already assigned)
      IF ls_entity-%tky-BoID IS INITIAL.
        " Generate ID if needed
        ls_db-boid = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ).
      ELSE.
        ls_db-boid = ls_entity-BoID.
      ENDIF.

      " Insert into database
      INSERT INTO zcomplexbo VALUES ls_db.
      IF sy-subrc <> 0.
        APPEND VALUE #( %cid = ls_entity-%cid ) TO failed-complexbo.
        APPEND VALUE #(
          %cid = ls_entity-%cid
          %msg = new_message_with_text(
            severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
            text     = |Insert failed for BO { ls_db-boid }| )
        ) TO reported-complexbo.
      ELSE.
        " Map %cid to actual key
        APPEND VALUE #(
          %cid = ls_entity-%cid
          BoID = ls_db-boid
        ) TO mapped-complexbo.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.


  METHOD update FOR MODIFY
    IMPORTING entities FOR ComplexBO.

    LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(ls_entity).
      " Read current DB state
      SELECT SINGLE * FROM zcomplexbo
        WHERE boid = @ls_entity-BoID
        INTO @DATA(ls_db).

      IF sy-subrc <> 0.
        APPEND VALUE #( BoID = ls_entity-BoID ) TO failed-complexbo.
        CONTINUE.
      ENDIF.

      " Apply only changed fields
      IF ls_entity-%control-Name = if_abap_behv=>mk-on.
        ls_db-name = ls_entity-Name.
      ENDIF.
      IF ls_entity-%control-Status = if_abap_behv=>mk-on.
        ls_db-status = ls_entity-Status.
      ENDIF.
      IF ls_entity-%control-Date = if_abap_behv=>mk-on.
        ls_db-date = ls_entity-Date.
      ENDIF.

      " Write back to database
      MODIFY zcomplexbo FROM ls_db.
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.


  METHOD delete FOR MODIFY
    IMPORTING keys FOR ComplexBO.

    LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key).
      " Check delete allowed
      SELECT SINGLE status FROM zcomplexbo
        WHERE boid = @ls_key-BoID
        INTO @DATA(lv_status).

      IF lv_status = 'CLOSED'.
        APPEND VALUE #( BoID = ls_key-BoID ) TO failed-complexbo.
        APPEND VALUE #(
          %tky = ls_key-%tky
          %msg = new_message_with_text(
            severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
            text     = |Cannot delete BO in CLOSED status| )
        ) TO reported-complexbo.
        CONTINUE.
      ENDIF.

      DELETE FROM zcomplexbo WHERE boid = @ls_key-BoID.
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

sections.codeBlock:
" CREATE — manual implementation
METHOD create FOR INSERT
  IMPORTING entities FOR ComplexBO.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(ls_entity).
    DATA(ls_db) = CORRESPONDING #( ls_entity ).
    IF ls_entity-%tky-BoID IS INITIAL.
      ls_db-boid = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ).
    ENDIF.
    INSERT INTO zcomplexbo VALUES ls_db.
    IF sy-subrc = 0.
      APPEND VALUE #( %cid = ls_entity-%cid BoID = ls_db-boid
      ) TO mapped-complexbo.
    ELSE.
      APPEND VALUE #( %cid = ls_entity-%cid ) TO failed-complexbo.
    ENDIF.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

" UPDATE — apply only changed fields via %control
METHOD update FOR MODIFY
  IMPORTING entities FOR ComplexBO.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(ls_upd).
    SELECT SINGLE * FROM zcomplexbo
      WHERE boid = @ls_upd-BoID INTO @DATA(ls_db).
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      APPEND VALUE #( BoID = ls_upd-BoID ) TO failed-complexbo.
      CONTINUE.
    ENDIF.
    IF ls_upd-%control-Name = if_abap_behv=>mk-on.
      ls_db-name = ls_upd-Name.
    ENDIF.
    IF ls_upd-%control-Status = if_abap_behv=>mk-on.
      ls_db-status = ls_upd-Status.
    ENDIF.
    MODIFY zcomplexbo FROM ls_db.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

" DELETE
METHOD delete FOR DELETE
  IMPORTING keys FOR ComplexBO.
  LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key).
    DELETE FROM zcomplexbo WHERE boid = ls_key-BoID.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Development Cost vs Flexibility Tradeoff in Unmanaged CRUD

variables.name: Entity complexity
variables.source: Simple = 1 table, Medium = 3 tables, Complex = legacy BAPI

managed.monthlyPenalty: $36,000 — Managed: 10 entities × avg 3 days × $1,200/day = $36K. Draft + locking included. 80% less code.

unmanaged.monthlyPenalty: $132,000 — Unmanaged: 10 entities × avg 11 days × $1,200/day = $132K. Every CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE + locking + draft + save must be implemented manually.

calculation.breakeven: Unmanaged is 3.7x more expensive in development. It only makes financial sense when >60% of operations are custom — the managed framework would require so many workarounds that the productivity advantage disappears.

sections.title: Custom Locking

description.value:
In RAP unmanaged scenario, you have full control over locking. The framework does not automatically manage ENQUEUE calls — you must implement them manually. This is both a burden and an opportunity: you can implement sophisticated locking strategies that go beyond simple entity-level locking.

Lock Object Integration: SAP's classic lock objects (created via transaction SM31) expose function modules ENQUEUE_* and DEQUEUE_*. In unmanaged scenario, you call these explicitly in your behavior pool methods. The typical pattern is: (1) Lock in the FOR MODIFY or early number method. (2) Commit or rollback the lock in the save method. (3) Unlock in case of failure.

CDS-Based Locking: Even in unmanaged scenario, you can use CDS-based locking by referencing a CDS entity that has @Lock annotations. The framework will then automatically manage basic record-level locking for that entity. However, you lose this benefit if you are using custom persistence (non-CDS tables).

Staff+ Insight: Custom locking in unmanaged RAP is the same pattern as pessimistic locking in any transaction system. FAANG engineers implement this with database-level locking (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE) or distributed locks (Redis Redlock, ZooKeeper). The principles are identical: acquire lock before modification, hold lock for minimum duration, release lock after commit.

CLASS zbp_rap_unmanaged IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD lock FOR LOCK
    IMPORTING keys TO LOCK ComplexBO.

    " Custom lock implementation
    " Called before MODIFY operations to acquire locks

    LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key).
      CALL FUNCTION 'ENQUEUE_EZ_COMPLEXBO'
        EXPORTING
          mode_zcomplexbo = 'E'
          mandt          = sy-mandt
          boid           = ls_key-BoID
          _scope         = '1'  " Lock for current LUW
        EXCEPTIONS
          foreign_lock   = 1
          system_failure = 2
          OTHERS         = 3.

      CASE sy-subrc.
        WHEN 1.
          " Lock held by another user
          APPEND VALUE #( BoID = ls_key-BoID ) TO failed-complexbo.
          APPEND VALUE #(
            %tky = ls_key-%tky
            %msg = new_message_with_text(
              severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
              text     = |BO { ls_key-BoID } is locked by another user| )
            %element-BoID = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
          ) TO reported-complexbo.

        WHEN 2.
          " System failure in lock management
          APPEND VALUE #( BoID = ls_key-BoID ) TO failed-complexbo.
          APPEND VALUE #(
            %tky = ls_key-%tky
            %msg = new_message_with_text(
              severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
              text     = |Lock system failure for BO { ls_key-BoID }| )
          ) TO reported-complexbo.
      ENDCASE.
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.


  METHOD unlock FOR UNLOCK
    IMPORTING keys TO UNLOCK ComplexBO.

    " Release locks on transaction completion
    LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key).
      CALL FUNCTION 'DEQUEUE_EZ_COMPLEXBO'
        EXPORTING
          mode_zcomplexbo = 'E'
          mandt          = sy-mandt
          boid           = ls_key-BoID.
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

sections.codeBlock:
" Custom lock implementation
METHOD lock FOR LOCK
  IMPORTING keys TO LOCK ComplexBO.
  LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key).
    CALL FUNCTION 'ENQUEUE_EZ_COMPLEXBO'
      EXPORTING
        mode_zcomplexbo = 'E'
        mandt  = sy-mandt
        boid   = ls_key-BoID
        _scope = '1'
      EXCEPTIONS
        foreign_lock   = 1
        system_failure = 2.
    CASE sy-subrc.
      WHEN 1.
        APPEND VALUE #( BoID = ls_key-BoID ) TO failed-complexbo.
      WHEN 2.
        APPEND VALUE #( BoID = ls_key-BoID ) TO failed-complexbo.
    ENDCASE.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

METHOD unlock FOR UNLOCK
  IMPORTING keys TO UNLOCK ComplexBO.
  LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key).
    CALL FUNCTION 'DEQUEUE_EZ_COMPLEXBO'
      EXPORTING mode_zcomplexbo = 'E'
                 mandt = sy-mandt
                 boid  = ls_key-BoID.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Our custom locking in unmanaged RAP is causing frequent timeouts. Users report 'Object locked by user XYZ' even when that user's session ended hours ago."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Increase the lock timeout parameter." (treats symptom, not cause)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "Lock timeouts from stale locks indicate the enqueue was never released. This is a classic lock leak pattern. In unmanaged RAP, you must explicitly release locks via DEQUEUE calls — the framework does not auto-release them like managed scenario. The three most common causes: (1) The lock is acquired in the MODIFY method but not released in the error path. If an exception occurs after ENQUEUE but before SAVE, the DEQUEUE never runs. Wrap ENQUEUE calls in TRY/CATCH with CLEANUP block that calls DEQUEUE. (2) The user's ABAP session terminated abnormally (program dump, network disconnect). SAP's enqueue server has a 'lock timeout' (enque/delay_roll, default 60s) that cleans up locks from terminated sessions — but this only works if the enqueue entry has the correct _scope parameter. Ensure _scope = '1' (rollback release) not '2' (manual release). (3) You're using SAP standard lock objects but the lock module ENQUEUE_EZ_* requires a RFC destination that's unavailable. Check SM12 for orphaned lock entries and implement a cleanup job that runs every 5 minutes to release locks older than 30 minutes."
adversarialResponse.pattern: root-cause-triage — identify specific failure modes (error-path leaks, session termination, lock scope) with precise SAP parameter references, and provide both immediate cleanup and long-term prevention strategies.

sections.title: Transaction Control

description.value:
Transaction control is where unmanaged scenario differs most dramatically from managed. In managed scenario, the framework controls the transaction boundary — you never call COMMIT WORK or ROLLBACK WORK. In unmanaged scenario, you must implement the save method and explicitly control the database commit.

The SAVE Sequence: The framework calls your save method when the user triggers the SAVE operation. This is where you: (1) Write all CREATE/UPDATE/DELETE changes to the database. (2) Call COMMIT WORK (or let the framework do it for you). (3) Handle any database exceptions. The framework provides two SAVE methods: save (called with the collected changes) and finalize_save (called after successful commit to update the buffer state).

LUW Boundaries: A LUW (Logical Unit of Work) in unmanaged RAP spans from the first MODIFY call to the final SAVE/COMMIT. Within this boundary, all changes are atomic — either all succeed or all fail. You can intervene at the SAVE point to validate cross-entity constraints before committing. If validation fails, you raise an error via FAILED and the commit is prevented.

Important Constraint: You cannot call COMMIT WORK directly in your behavior pool methods (CREATE, UPDATE, DELETE) — only in the save method. The framework enforces this to prevent partial commits. If you need to commit intermediate work (e.g., calling a BAPI that does its own commit), you must handle this with special care — typically by wrapping the BAPI call in an RFC with destination 'NONE' to isolate the transaction.

CLASS zbp_rap_unmanaged IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD save FOR SAVE
    IMPORTING keys TO SAVE ComplexBO.

    " The SAVE sequence:
    " 1. The framework has already called create/update/delete
    " 2. Now it calls save() to persist the changes
    " 3. You must flush your changes to the database here
    " 4. Then the framework calls COMMIT WORK

    DATA: lt_db_updates TYPE TABLE OF zcomplexbo.

    " Example: Do any final validation before commit
    LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key).
      SELECT SINGLE * FROM zcomplexbo
        WHERE boid = @ls_key-BoID
        INTO @DATA(ls_db).

      IF ls_db-amount > ls_db-credit_limit.
        APPEND VALUE #( BoID = ls_key-BoID ) TO failed-complexbo.
        APPEND VALUE #(
          %tky = ls_key-%tky
          %msg = new_message_with_text(
            severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
            text     = |Amount exceeds credit limit| )
        ) TO reported-complexbo.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

    " If no failures, the framework proceeds to COMMIT.
    " If failures exist, the framework ROLLBACKs.

  ENDMETHOD.


  METHOD finalize_save FOR FINALIZE SAVE
    IMPORTING keys TO FINALIZE ComplexBO.

    " Called AFTER successful COMMIT.
    " Use this to update cache, send notifications, etc.
    " At this point, data is committed to database.

    LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key).
      " Send confirmation email, update cache, etc.
      " Do NOT modify database here!
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.


  METHOD early_save FOR EARLY SAVE
    IMPORTING keys OF STRUCTURE ComplexBO.

    " Called before the main SAVE sequence.
    " Use for pre-save cleanup or preparatory work.
    " If this fails, the entire save sequence is aborted.

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

sections.codeBlock:
" SAVE method — final validation before COMMIT
METHOD save FOR SAVE
  IMPORTING keys TO SAVE ComplexBO.
  LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key).
    SELECT SINGLE * FROM zcomplexbo
      WHERE boid = @ls_key-BoID INTO @DATA(ls_db).
    IF ls_db-amount > ls_db-credit_limit.
      APPEND VALUE #( BoID = ls_key-BoID ) TO failed-complexbo.
      APPEND VALUE #(
        %tky = ls_key-%tky
        %msg = new_message_with_text(
          severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
          text = |Amount exceeds credit limit| )
      ) TO reported-complexbo.
    ENDIF.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

METHOD finalize_save FOR FINALIZE SAVE
  IMPORTING keys TO FINALIZE ComplexBO.
  " Called AFTER successful COMMIT
  " Send notification, update cache — do NOT modify DB here
ENDMETHOD.

METHOD early_save FOR EARLY SAVE
  IMPORTING keys OF STRUCTURE ComplexBO.
  " Pre-save cleanup — abort saves if this fails
ENDMETHOD.

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Transaction Control — Framework-Managed vs Custom LUW Boundary

managed_luw.monthlyPenalty: $0 — Framework-managed LUW: auto-commit, auto-rollback, auto-buffering. 0 development cost for transaction control. Cannot integrate BAPIs that commit independently.

unmanaged_luw.monthlyPenalty: $2,400 — Unmanaged: 50ms BAPI time + 50ms ABAP processing + 50ms DB commit = 150ms per save. Development cost: ~$12K for save/finalize_save/early_save implementation.

calculation.breakeven: Managed is always cheaper for pure-SAP scenarios. Unmanaged only adds value when BAPI integration is required — the cost of working around managed's transaction limitations exceeds the unmanaged implementation cost.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Our unmanaged RAP transaction control is unreliable. Sometimes data is saved even when validation fails."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Check your validation code." (dismissive, no systematic approach)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "This is a critical issue — partial saves with failed validations indicate the save sequence is not properly halting on errors. Let me identify the likely causes: (1) You're calling COMMIT WORK in the create/update methods instead of deferring to the save method. In unmanaged RAP, only the save method should trigger database commits. If you call COMMIT in create, the data is committed before validations run. Move all DB writes to the save method. (2) Your save method doesn't check the failed table before proceeding. The framework checks failed before committing, but if you bypass the framework by calling COMMIT WORK inside save, you override this safeguard. Never call COMMIT WORK directly — let the framework handle it. (3) You have an early_save method that writes to the database but doesn't participate in rollback. If early_save fails, data written to the database is NOT automatically rolled back. Make early_save idempotent and use temporary tables that can be cleaned up on failure. (4) BAPI calls that commit internally are creating nested LUWs. Isolate BAPI calls with RFC destination 'NONE' to prevent them from committing your transaction context."
adversarialResponse.pattern: audit-and-fix — identify the specific transaction boundary violations (early COMMIT, unchecked failures, BAPI nesting) with exact code patterns to avoid, and provide a save sequence checklist.

sections.title: Draft Hand-Written Implementation

description.value:
Implementing draft handling in unmanaged scenario is the most complex part of the RAP unmanaged API. In managed scenario, drafts are automatic. In unmanaged scenario, you must implement the entire draft lifecycle: draft creation, draft persistence, draft activation, and draft discard. The framework provides the infrastructure (draft table structure, draft action handlers), but the actual data movement between draft and active storage is your responsibility.

The 4 Internal Draft Tables: Behind every RAP draft are 4 internal tables maintained by the framework: (1) Header Table — the draft header record (DraftID, ParentDraftID, creation timestamp, etc.). (2) Item Table — the actual business data (the fields of your entity). (3) Control Table — tracks which fields have been modified (%control flags). (4) %is_draft — a flag indicating whether a record is a draft or active. These tables are managed by the framework in managed scenario; in unmanaged scenario, you must persist the draft data yourself and ensure the framework's internal bookkeeping is consistent.

Draft Activation Flow: (1) The framework reads the draft data from your draft persistence. (2) It passes the draft data to your save method (like a normal CREATE/UPDATE). (3) You write the data to the active tables. (4) You delete the draft record. (5) The framework updates its internal draft registry.

Staff+ Insight: Writing hand-implemented drafts is the RAP equivalent of implementing a change data capture (CDC) system. You are maintaining two data stores (draft and active) and ensuring they remain consistent. This is the same pattern as event sourcing's write-ahead log and materialized views.

<svg viewBox="0 0 700 400" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.drft-box{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:var(--brand-primary);stroke-width:2;rx:8}.drft-tbl{fill:var(--bg-secondary);stroke:var(--text-primary);stroke-width:2;rx:6}.drft-flow{fill:none;stroke:var(--brand-primary);stroke-width:2;marker-end:url(#arrow)}.drft-back{fill:none;stroke:var(--border-default);stroke-width:1.5;stroke-dasharray:4;animation:flowDash 1.5s linear infinite}.drft-lbl{fill:var(--text-primary);font-family:Ubuntu,system-ui;font-size:11px;font-weight:600;text-anchor:middle}.drft-sub{fill:var(--text-primary);font-family:Ubuntu,system-ui;font-size:9px;text-anchor:middle}.ann-pulse{animation:pulse 2s ease-in-out infinite}@keyframes pulse{0%,100%{opacity:.6}50%{opacity:1}}@keyframes flowDash{0%{stroke-dashoffset:-8}100%{stroke-dashoffset:0}}</style><defs><marker id="arrow" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="6" refX="8" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L8,3 L0,6 Z" fill="var(--brand-primary)"/></marker></defs><rect class="drft-box" x="15" y="15" width="670" height="370"/><text class="drft-lbl" x="350" y="42">Unmanaged Draft Persistence Architecture</text><rect class="drft-box" x="30" y="65" width="290" height="100"/><text class="drft-lbl" x="175" y="85">Framework Draft Tables</text><text class="drft-sub" x="175" y="105">Header: DraftID, timestamps, status</text><text class="drft-sub" x="175" y="120">Control: %control flags (modified fields)</text><text class="drft-sub" x="175" y="135">%is_draft flag</text><rect class="drft-tbl" x="380" y="65" width="290" height="100"/><text class="drft-lbl" x="525" y="85">Your Draft Persistence</text><text class="drft-sub" x="525" y="105">ZDRAFT_COMPLEXBO (header + data)</text><text class="drft-sub" x="525" y="120">Must store all business fields</text><text class="drft-sub" x="525" y="135">Must store original active record key</text><line x1="320" y1="115" x2="380" y2="115" class="drft-flow"/><line x1="175" y1="165" x2="175" y2="200" class="drft-flow"/><line x1="525" y1="165" x2="525" y2="200" class="drft-flow"/><rect class="drft-box" x="30" y="200" width="290" height="60"/><text class="drft-lbl" x="175" y="222">ACTIVATE Action Handler</text><text class="drft-sub" x="175" y="238">Read draft -> validate -> move to active</text><rect class="drft-tbl" x="380" y="200" width="290" height="60"/><text class="drft-lbl" x="525" y="222">DISCARD Action Handler</text><text class="drft-sub" x="525" y="238">Delete draft record, no DB write</text><line x1="175" y1="260" x2="175" y2="290" class="drft-flow"/><line x1="525" y1="260" x2="525" y2="290" class="drft-flow"/><rect class="drft-tbl" x="180" y="290" width="340" height="55"/><text class="drft-lbl" x="350" y="312">Active Table (ZCOMPLEXBO)</text><text class="drft-sub" x="350" y="328">Data after successful activation</text></svg>

CLASS zbp_rap_unmanaged IMPLEMENTATION.

  " Draft action: Create a new draft
  METHOD draft_create FOR DRAFT CREATE
    IMPORTING entities FOR ComplexBO.

    DATA: ls_draft TYPE zdraft_complexbo.

    LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(ls_entity).
      MOVE-CORRESPONDING ls_entity TO ls_draft.
      ls_draft-draft_id = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ).
      ls_draft-is_draft = abap_true.

      INSERT INTO zdraft_complexbo VALUES ls_draft.

      APPEND VALUE #(
        %cid = ls_entity-%cid
        BoID = ls_draft-boid
      ) TO mapped-complexbo.
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.


  " Draft action: Resume editing an existing draft
  METHOD draft_resume FOR DRAFT RESUME
    IMPORTING keys FOR ComplexBO.

    " Read draft data and return to framework
    READ ENTITIES OF Z_ComplexBO IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY ComplexBO
      FROM VALUE #( FOR key IN keys ( BoID = key-BoID ) )
      RESULT DATA(lt_draft).

    " Framework takes over and populates the edit UI
  ENDMETHOD.


  " Draft action: Discard a draft
  METHOD draft_discard FOR DRAFT DISCARD
    IMPORTING keys FOR ComplexBO.

    LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key).
      DELETE FROM zdraft_complexbo
        WHERE boid = @ls_key-BoID
        AND is_draft = @abap_true.
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.


  " Draft action: Activate a draft (save to active table)
  METHOD draft_activate FOR DRAFT ACTIVATE
    IMPORTING keys FOR ComplexBO.

    LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_act_key).
      " Read draft data
      SELECT SINGLE * FROM zdraft_complexbo
        WHERE boid = @ls_act_key-BoID
        INTO @DATA(ls_draft).

      IF sy-subrc <> 0.
        APPEND VALUE #( BoID = ls_act_key-BoID ) TO failed-complexbo.
        CONTINUE.
      ENDIF.

      " Write to active table (upsert)
      MOVE-CORRESPONDING ls_draft TO DATA(ls_active).
      ls_active-is_draft = abap_false.
      MODIFY zcomplexbo FROM ls_active.

      " Delete the draft record
      DELETE FROM zdraft_complexbo
        WHERE boid = @ls_act_key-BoID.
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

sections.codeBlock:
" Draft: CREATE
METHOD draft_create FOR DRAFT CREATE
  IMPORTING entities FOR ComplexBO.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(ls_entity).
    DATA(ls_draft) = CORRESPONDING zdraft_complexbo( ls_entity ).
    ls_draft-draft_id = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_x16_static( ).
    ls_draft-is_draft = abap_true.
    INSERT INTO zdraft_complexbo VALUES ls_draft.
    APPEND VALUE #( %cid = ls_entity-%cid BoID = ls_draft-boid
    ) TO mapped-complexbo.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

" Draft: DISCARD
METHOD draft_discard FOR DRAFT DISCARD
  IMPORTING keys FOR ComplexBO.
  DELETE FROM zdraft_complexbo FOR ALL ENTRIES IN keys
    WHERE boid = keys-BoID AND is_draft = abap_true.
ENDMETHOD.

" Draft: ACTIVATE (save draft to active table)
METHOD draft_activate FOR DRAFT ACTIVATE
  IMPORTING keys FOR ComplexBO.
  LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key).
    SELECT SINGLE * FROM zdraft_complexbo
      WHERE boid = @ls_key-BoID INTO @DATA(ls_draft).
    CHECK sy-subrc = 0.
    DATA(ls_active) = CORRESPONDING zcomplexbo( ls_draft ).
    ls_active-is_draft = abap_false.
    MODIFY zcomplexbo FROM ls_active.
    DELETE FROM zdraft_complexbo WHERE boid = ls_key-BoID.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

decisionTree.prompt: Should I implement custom draft handling in unmanaged RAP?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Does your business object need draft support?" If users need to save incomplete work and resume later, you need draft. If all operations are complete-and-done (batch, API-to-API), you don't need draft.

branches.condition: Fiori UI requires draft for user experience
branches.path: Implement custom draft. Must implement draft_create, draft_resume, draft_activate, draft_discard. Requires 4 methods and a dedicated draft persistence table. ~200 lines of code minimum.
Skip draft. Use direct create/update/delete without draft lifecycle. Simpler, faster, no draft table cleanup needed. Not suitable for Fiori UIs.

branches.condition: Need Fiori draft but unmanaged complexity is too high
branches.path: Consider managed scenario instead. If 80%+ of your use case fits managed, use managed for the draft lifecycle and implement only custom logic via determinations/actions.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Custom draft in unmanaged is the most complex RAP implementation. If you need Fiori UI support, consider whether you can switch to managed scenario for the draft layer alone.

sections.title: Integration with Existing BAPIs/RFCs

description.value:
One of the primary reasons to choose unmanaged scenario is the need to integrate with existing BAPIs, RFCs, or legacy function modules. In many SAP landscapes, core business logic is encapsulated in BAPIs (Business Application Programming Interfaces) that have been stable for decades. Replacing these BAPIs with RAP managed scenario is often impractical or impossible.

Integration Pattern: The fundamental pattern is: (1) The RAP UI (Fiori) sends a request to the RAP runtime. (2) The runtime calls your unmanaged behavior pool method. (3) Your method calls the BAPI/RFC with the appropriate parameters. (4) The BAPI performs the business logic and commits (or returns an error). (5) You handle the BAPI's return messages (BAPIRET2) and map them to RAP's reported/failed structures. (6) You handle the fact that the BAPI may have already committed the work (BAPI_COMMIT).

Key Challenge — Transaction Boundary: BAPIs typically call COMMIT WORK internally via BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT. This breaks RAP's transaction assumption that only the SAVE method commits. The solution is to use RFC with destination 'NONE' (same LUW) or to adjust your architecture: (1) Call the BAPI in the save method. (2) The BAPI does its internal commit. (3) You prevent the framework from doing a second commit (by setting set_update_state).

Message Mapping: BAPIs return messages via a BAPIRET2 table. You must map these to RAP's reported structure. The mapping is: message type E/A → failed table entry + reported error message. Message type W → reported warning. Message type S → reported success. Message type I → reported info. The mapping ensures that Fiori UIs display the BAPI messages correctly.

CLASS zbp_rap_unmanaged IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD create FOR INSERT
    IMPORTING entities FOR ComplexBO.

    DATA: lt_bapi_return TYPE TABLE OF bapiret2.
    DATA: ls_bapi_header TYPE bapi_orders_create_header.
    DATA: lv_sales_doc TYPE bapivbeln-vbeln.

    LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(ls_entity).
      " Map RAP entity fields to BAPI structure
      ls_bapi_header = CORRESPONDING #( ls_entity ).

      " Call the legacy BAPI
      CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_SALESORDER_CREATEFROMDAT2'
        EXPORTING
          order_header_in  = ls_bapi_header
        IMPORTING
          salesdocument    = lv_sales_doc
        TABLES
          return           = lt_bapi_return.

      " Check BAPI return messages
      DATA(lv_error) = abap_false.
      LOOP AT lt_bapi_return INTO DATA(ls_return)
          WHERE type = 'A' OR type = 'E'.
        lv_error = abap_true.

        APPEND VALUE #( %cid = ls_entity-%cid ) TO failed-complexbo.
        APPEND VALUE #(
          %cid = ls_entity-%cid
          %msg = new_message_with_text(
            severity = COND #(
              WHEN ls_return-type = 'A' THEN if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
              WHEN ls_return-type = 'E' THEN if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
              ELSE if_abap_behv_message=>severity-success )
            text = |{ ls_return-message }| )
        ) TO reported-complexbo.
      ENDLOOP.

      IF lv_error = abap_false.
        " BAPI succeeded - commit the BAPI work
        CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT'
          EXPORTING
            wait = abap_true.

        " Map result to RAP response
        APPEND VALUE #(
          %cid = ls_entity-%cid
          BoID = lv_sales_doc
        ) TO mapped-complexbo.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.


  METHOD save FOR SAVE
    IMPORTING keys TO SAVE ComplexBO.

    " BAPI may have already committed, so
    " prevent the framework from double-committing
    LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key).
      " The framework's set_update_state can be used
      " to tell the runtime that the commit is already done
    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.


  " Helper: Map BAPIRET2 to RAP message
  METHOD map_bapi_message.
    DATA: lr_msg TYPE REF TO if_abap_behv_message.

    lr_msg = new_message_with_text(
      severity = SWITCH #(
        is_return-type
        WHEN 'E' THEN if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
        WHEN 'W' THEN if_abap_behv_message=>severity-warning
        WHEN 'S' THEN if_abap_behv_message=>severity-success
        WHEN 'I' THEN if_abap_behv_message=>severity-info
        ELSE if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error )
      text = is_return-message ).

    rv_msg = lr_msg.
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

sections.codeBlock:
" CREATE via BAPI integration
METHOD create FOR INSERT
  IMPORTING entities FOR ComplexBO.
  DATA: lt_bapi_return TYPE TABLE OF bapiret2.
  LOOP AT entities INTO DATA(ls_entity).
    DATA(ls_bapi_header) = CORRESPONDING bapi_orders_create_header( ls_entity ).
    CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_SALESORDER_CREATEFROMDAT2'
      EXPORTING order_header_in = ls_bapi_header
      IMPORTING salesdocument = DATA(lv_sales_doc)
      TABLES return = lt_bapi_return.
    DATA(lv_error) = abap_false.
    LOOP AT lt_bapi_return INTO DATA(ls_return) WHERE type = 'A' OR type = 'E'.
      lv_error = abap_true.
      APPEND VALUE #( %cid = ls_entity-%cid ) TO failed-complexbo.
      APPEND VALUE #(
        %cid = ls_entity-%cid
        %msg = new_message_with_text(
          severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
          text = ls_return-message ) ) TO reported-complexbo.
    ENDLOOP.
    IF lv_error = abap_false.
      CALL FUNCTION 'BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT' EXPORTING wait = abap_true.
      APPEND VALUE #( %cid = ls_entity-%cid BoID = lv_sales_doc
      ) TO mapped-complexbo.
    ENDIF.
  ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD.

" SAVE: prevent double-commit after BAPI
METHOD save FOR SAVE
  IMPORTING keys TO SAVE ComplexBO.
  " BAPI already committed — prevent framework double-commit
  " set_update_state( if_abap_behv=>update_state-committed )
ENDMETHOD.

decisionTree.prompt: How should I integrate existing BAPIs into RAP unmanaged scenario?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Does the BAPI commit internally or expect the caller to commit?" If the BAPI calls BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT internally, you must handle double-commit prevention. If it only fills BAPIRET2 tables, you control the commit via the RAP save sequence.

branches.condition: BAPI calls COMMIT internally (most legacy BAPIs)
branches.path: Call BAPI in create/update method, then call BAPI_TRANSACTION_COMMIT. Prevent framework double-commit via set_update_state( if_abap_behv=>update_state-committed ) in save method.

branches.condition: BAPI returns data, caller decides when to commit
branches.path: Call BAPI in create/update method. Defer COMMIT to RAP save sequence. Map BAPIRET2 messages to failed/reported structures in the save method.
Call in determination or validation. No commit handling needed. Map return messages to reported structure for user feedback.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: BAPI integration in unmanaged RAP creates a transaction boundary challenge. The BAPI's internal commit and RAP's framework commit are two separate LUWs — you must prevent double-commit explicitly.

details: Staff+ Insight: Choosing managed vs unmanaged is the kind of architectural tradeoff FAANG Staff+ interviews test. Frame it as: "Managed optimizes for developer productivity at the cost of flexibility. Unmanaged trades productivity for full control. I choose based on the complexity of business logic and the need for custom persistence."
